data.title: Allstate estimates $83M of catastrophe losses in October
data.description:
• The Allstate Corporation (ALL) announced on Thursday that its estimated catastrophe losses for October were $83M or $65M after-tax from five wind and hail events, down from catastrophe losses of $161M in September or $128M after-tax.
  • Allstate's protection policies in force increased 0.3% M/M and 1.4% Y/Y to 38.15M as of the end of October. Homeowners' policies in force increased 2% Y/Y to 7.66M, while commercial lines PIF dropped 23.2% Y/Y to 175K. Auto PIF gained 1.7% Y/Y to 25.42M.
  • ALL is +0.88% to $211.79.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Kulicke & Soffa anticipates 7% revenue increase in Q1 2026 amid leadership transition and technology ramp
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kulicke & Soffa Industries (KLIC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Lester Wong, Interim CEO and CFO, opened by addressing the recent leadership change following Fusen Chen's retirement, highlighting that he will continue as Executive VP of Finance & IT while the company searches for a permanent CEO. Wong stated the transition is expected to be seamless, with "continued innovation, global support and strong commitment from K&S to serve the evolving needs and to enable next-generation devices."
  • Wong acknowledged Chen's legacy of expanding into high-potential technologies and growing customer engagement, noting, "We have accelerated the growth of our advanced portfolio of solutions, which enabled meaningful share gains in leading edge logic and has paved the path for additional expansion in DRAM, power semiconductor and advanced dispense." He added that Chen will provide advisory support over the coming year.
  • Turning to results, Wong reported, "Within our fourth fiscal quarter, we generated revenue of $177.6 million, GAAP earnings per share of $0.12 and non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.28."
  • The company cited a "24% sequential" increase in general semiconductor revenue and a "nearly 60%" rise in memory-related revenue to $24.4 million, driven by NAND-related capacity additions. Utilization rates in these segments are currently over 80%.
  • Wong highlighted the upcoming shipment of the first HBM system in the December quarter and ongoing momentum in advanced packaging, advanced dispense, and power semiconductor. He stated, "Our operational and supply chain teams are actively preparing for a production ramp through fiscal 2026 as adoption for our FTC process begins to accelerate."
  • Wong detailed the launch of the ACELON dispense system, "released during Semiconductor Taiwan in September," and reported recurring and new purchase orders for advanced dispense systems.
  • Wong summarized, "We continue to expand our market presence on multiple fronts and remain cautiously optimistic as key regions and end markets show signs of cyclical improvement."
  • On financials, Wong added, "Gross margins came in at 45.7%, and we delivered $0.28 of non-GAAP earnings. Total operating expense came in at $80.3 million on a GAAP basis and just below $70 million on a non-GAAP basis."
  • Wong reported the company repurchased $16.7 million in shares during the quarter, totaling $96.5 million for the fiscal year.

Outlook

  • For the December quarter, Wong stated, "Revenue is expected to increase by approximately 7% sequentially to $190 million with gross margins at 47%. Non-GAAP operating expenses are expected to be $71 million with GAAP earnings per share targeted to be $0.18 and non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.33."
  • Looking into fiscal 2026, Wong said, "We anticipate that half of our incremental growth will stem from technology transitions and share gains in new markets. At the same time, the other portion of sequential growth is increasingly encouraging due to the anticipation of ongoing cyclical recovery over the coming quarters."

Financial Results

  • Wong reported $177.6 million in revenue, $0.12 in GAAP EPS, and $0.28 in non-GAAP EPS for the fourth quarter. Gross margin was 45.7%. Total operating expense was $80.3 million GAAP, below $70 million non-GAAP.
  • General semiconductor revenue rose 24% sequentially; memory-related revenue increased nearly 60% to $24.4 million. Utilization rates across key end markets are above 80%.
  • The company repurchased $16.7 million in shares this quarter and $96.5 million for the fiscal year.

Q&A

  • Sreekrishnan Sankarnarayanan, TD Cowen: Asked about the outlook for all three business segments and seasonality into the March quarter. Wong responded, "General semi and memory are actually very strong. Utilization for both is over 80%. Auto and Industrial is still lagging a little bit, but we do -- we're very optimistic about it because we do see improvements, and we think there will be sequential growth into Q1. So... March will probably be -- probably flat to Q1."
  • Sankarnarayanan, TD Cowen: Asked about competition in FTC plasma solutions. Wong replied, "We're still the only one at the foundry doing high-volume production... our FTC solution is basically best-in-class, and we feel very, very competitive."
  • Yu Shi, Needham: Inquired about the HBM system shipment and next milestones. Wong said, "We're shipping the system to... somewhere in the United States... once it's installed, they're going to start running wafers through it, and we're going to look for qualification."
  • Thomas Diffely, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the NAND market and order trends. Wong explained, "We're seeing very high utilization rates in memory... purchase orders increasing in that market as well, particularly in China."
  • David Duley, Steelhead Securities: Asked about increasing market share in DRAM and HBM shipment strategy. Wong clarified, "The slide referred to increasing market share in DRAM, not specifically HBM... we're just entering the HBM market now."
  • Craig Ellis, B. Riley: Sought details on the trajectory of memory market recovery. Wong stated, "I do think this is a ramp in memory, and it will continue into FY '26."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts generally expressed optimism about the company's technology transitions and end market improvements but raised recurring questions about competitive positioning, recovery trajectory, and market share. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, with some cautious probing around segment growth and execution timelines.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing operational readiness and growth opportunities. During Q&A, Wong maintained a positive yet measured tone, frequently referencing high utilization rates and competitive positioning, while providing concrete details on outlook and transition milestones.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to more direct confidence, especially regarding sequential growth and technology ramp. Analyst sentiment was also more upbeat, with less focus on macro headwinds and more on execution and market opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a major management change with the retirement of CEO Fusen Chen and appointment of Lester Wong as Interim CEO, which was not present in the previous quarter.
  • Sequential revenue increased from $148.4 million in Q3 to $177.6 million in Q4. GAAP EPS improved from (-$0.06) to $0.12, and non-GAAP EPS rose from $0.07 to $0.28.
  • Gross margin decreased from 46.7% to 45.7%. Operating expenses increased from $75.3 million to $80.3 million GAAP.
  • The outlook for the December quarter was more positive, with revenue expected to rise to $190 million, gross margin to 47%, and non-GAAP EPS to $0.33.
  • Management shifted from referencing persistent headwinds in automotive/industrial to expecting sequential improvements in those segments.
  • Analyst focus moved from concerns about macro uncertainty and seasonality to questions around technology ramp, competitive differentiation, and DRAM/HBM market share.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted continued order hesitation within automotive and industrial segments, though they now anticipate improvement in the December quarter.
  • Wong addressed ongoing competitive pressures in advanced packaging and HBM, reaffirming K&S's positioning as "best-in-class" and noting broad customer engagement.
  • The company continues to monitor tax expense, with the effective tax rate expected to remain above 20% in the near term.
  • No new risks or challenges, such as customer concentration or supply chain disruption, were explicitly discussed in this quarter's remarks.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized confidence in the business's strategic direction, highlighting leadership continuity, robust technology transitions, and improving end market dynamics. With a projected 7% sequential revenue increase to $190 million in the December quarter and ongoing expansion in advanced packaging and memory, the company signals readiness to capitalize on cyclical recovery and technology-driven growth heading into fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Verizon to slash over 13,000 jobs under new CEO's cost-cutting plan
data.description:
Verizon (VZ) has begun a massive layoff process that is expected to impact more than 13,000 employees, including retail workers, customer service representatives, and some senior executives, most of whom are based in the U.S., multiple media outlets reported Thursday, citing an internal memo from new chief executive Dan Schulman.

The layoffs will reduce the size of the company's non-union workforce by as much as 20%, and further restructuring is expected in the coming weeks, the media reports said. Only a few union-affiliated jobs will be impacted by the cuts.

"Our current cost structure limits our ability to invest significantly in our customer value proposition. We must reorient our entire company around delivering for and delighting our customers," Schulman reportedly said in the memo.

The company plans to convert 179 corporate-owned retail stores into franchised operations and close one NYC store, the media reports said.

Most of the affected employees will be off the company's payroll by the end of the year. The job cuts were not the result of the company’s use of AI, the reports said.

Verizon is also setting aside $20M for laid-off employees to focus on the "opportunities and necessary skill sets as we enter the age of AI," Schulman reportedly said.

data.title: Waymo launches autonomous rides in Tampa, New Orleans and, Minneapolis
data.description:
Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo announced that it will start offering fully autonomous freeway rides across Tampa, New Orleans and Minneapolis.

“Three more cities on the map! You may spot our cars on the road as we lay the early groundwork for our autonomous ride-hailing service,” said the firm in its social media post.

Earlier this week, the firm introduced fully autonomous driving in Miami, Dallas, Houston, San Antonio and Orlando.

The self-driving technology firm, in its blog, stated that operations in Miami starts today and will begin in the remaining four cities over the coming weeks, ahead of opening its doors to riders next year.

The firm started offering fully autonomous freeway rides last week across the San Francisco Bay Area, Phoenix, and Los Angeles.

Waymo's services are currently operational in the San Francisco Bay Area, Los Angeles, Phoenix, Austin, and Atlanta and the firm plans to launch in Boston, Seattle, Denver, Miami, New York City, and Washington, DC.

data.title: Ivanhoe Mines opens long-awaited South African platinum mine
data.description:
Ivanhoe Mines (OTCQX:IVPAF) has formally inaugurated its long-awaited Platreef mine in South Africa, which could become one of the world's biggest platinum producers if plans to eventually supply more than 1 million oz/year are realized, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

Platreef is planned to ramp up production to 100,000 oz/year of platinum, palladium, rhodium and gold, before targeting 450,000 oz/year in its second phase from ~2028.

Platreef's history shows how it can take decades to transform promising geology into operating mines: The project was first conceived in the 1980s, before Ivanhoe’s (OTCQX:IVPAF) subsidiary received a prospecting permit in 2000 and converted that to mining rights in 2014.

The new mine begiins production as platinum and palladium have rallied this year, rising as much as 71% and 54%, respectively, following a protracted market slump.

data.title: Nasdaq jumps over 2% on Nvidia results and strong Sept jobs data
data.description:
U.S. stocks jumped up on Thursday after Nvidia’s (NVDA) results reset the market’s risk tone for the time being. Adding to the momentum, the long-awaited September jobs report finally landed, delivering payroll growth that easily topped expectations.

Leading the charge higher on the session was the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) as it was last up by 2.2%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had pushed up by 1.6% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed by 1.2%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, all of the 11 S&P segments find themselves up in positive territory and are led by Info Tech. Going in the other direction, Health Care has been the weakest performer thus far.

As for stocks that were on the move, Nvidia was +3.9% after adding more than $200B to its market cap with its post-earnings rally, nearing the $200/share level it lost early this month.

Thursday's spotlight was on the September nonfarm payrolls report, which was initially supposed to come out on October 3. However, the longest U.S. government shutdown in history derailed that release.

The data showed the U.S. labor market added 119K jobs in September, easily outstripping the consensus of 50K. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in the month, a tad higher than the expected 4.3%.

“Thursday's jobs report was much stronger than expected and it's possible that the Federal Reserve may take more of a wait and see approach to rates in December, especially since there is still uncertainty over the economic data, which has only first started to come back online after the drought from the government shutdown. Thursday's jobs report suggests that the labor market is not as weak as feared,” Alexander Guiliano, chief investment officer, Resonate Wealth Partners stated.

Moreover, Treasury yields shifted lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 3 basis points to 4.11% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 3 basis points to 3.56%.

data.title: Woodward announces new $1.8B share repurchase program
data.description:
• Woodward (WWD) approved a new $1.8B, three-year share repurchase authorization.
  • The company completed its prior $600M buyback authorization early, finishing in November 2025.
  • Over the past decade, the company has returned about $2 billion to shareholders, equal to roughly 78% of net earnings.

data.title: Marvell to boost hiring in India amid AI spending boom: report
data.description:
• Marvell Technology (MRVL) is set to boost its hiring in India amid the artificial intelligence spending boom, a company executive told Reuters. 
  • Marvell executive Navin Bishnoi told the news outlet that Marvell will grow its workforce in India, already at 1,700 employees, by 15% annually over the next three years.
  • “India is now probably the third largest in data center footprint,” Bishnoi said during the interview. He added that the company, which makes application specific integrated circuits, is in talks with various customers, such as hyperscalers and local companies, to increase its client base. Marvell already makes Amazon's (AMZN) Trainium chips.
  • Marvell shares rose 3% in early trading on Wednesday.

data.title: Voyager Technologies acquires Estes Energetics
data.description:
• Voyager Technologies (VOYG) acquired Estes Energetics, a U.S. leader in energetics, propulsion materials and critical missile-defense compounds.
  • The acquisition gives Voyager end-to-end control over production, quality, and certification of energetics materials.
  • The deal creates full vertical integration from raw materials to ballistic release, strengthening the U.S. munitions industrial base and supply-chain resilience.
  • VOYG shares up 4.8% on Thursday.

data.title: Tech bubble isn’t due to Nvidia valuations but AI spending – analyst
data.description:
David Bahnsen, chief investment officer at The Bahnsen Group, is raising concerns about a potential bubble in the technology sector (VGT), (IYW), (XLK) specifically focusing on massive AI-related expenditures rather than Nvidia’s (NVDA) soaring revenue.

During an interview with CNBC, Bahnsen emphasized that the real bubble risk lies with hyperscalers committing to enormous capital expenditures without clear returns on investment.

“I think the biggest part of the bubble for me is not he who is receiving the money, which is Nvidia (NVDA). It’s those who are paying the money, which are the hyperscalers who have seen their cap-ex level go to just stratospheric levels, and I think that’s the bigger problem,” he said.

He pointed to what he described as “bubble-like behavior” in the tech sector (VGT), (IYW), (XLK), with companies making substantial financial commitments without clarity on their potential returns.

Bahnsen expressed particular concern about the sustainability of these investments, noting: “I’m worried about the ROI on those expenditures. I don’t think that any of the hyperscalers have any idea what the ROI is, and that’s fine to a certain degree, but not now at $1.5T of five-year, six-year pro forma commitments.”

This perspective stands in stark contrast to the generally bullish market sentiment following Nvidia’s (NVDA) recent performance.

As a self-described “fundamental oriented investor,” Bahnsen emphasized the importance of cash flow-driven investment strategies in the current market environment.

“When you’re dividend growth driven, reasonable is often connected to cash flow and growth of cash flow,” he explained, adding that many tech companies previously thrived because they were “capital light” but are now facing significant AI capital expenditure commitments.

He advocated for investments in companies with strong free cash flow positions that can return value to shareholders.

“I really do like companies that are in a free cash flow position to return a lot to shareholders and reward us and derisk our investments,” he noted, describing this approach as “a reasonable way to generate handsome profits in a risk-adjusted way for investors.”
